rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 11, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 2. Defendant Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company's Motion for Summary Adjudication is GRANTED, in part, DENIED, in part. Opposition filed. Reply filed. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Frederick J. Ayers ("Decedent") while working at Hunters Point Shipyard and Mare Island Naval Shipyard between 1961-1976, was exposed to asbestos containing replacement gaskets, Cranite and Goodyearite, manufactured by Defendant Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company ("Defendant.") Defendant seeks summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation, Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure, and claim for punitive damages. A defendant seeking summary judgment or adjudication must "conclusively negate a necessary element of the plaintiff's case, or ... demonstrate that under no hypothesis is there a material issue of fact that requires the process of trial." (Guz v. Bechtel Nat. Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 317, 334.) To show that a plaintiff cannot establish an element of a cause of action, a defendant must make the initial showing "that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854.) "The defendant may, but need not, present evidence that conclusively negates an element of the plaintiff's cause of action. The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence - as through admissions by the plaintiff following extensive discovery to the effect that he has discovered nothing." (Id. at p. 855.) 'If plaintiffs respond to comprehensive interrogatories seeking all known facts with boilerplate answers that restate their allegations, or simply provide laundry lists of people and/or documents, the burden of production will almost certainly be shifted to them once defendants move for summary judgment and properly present plaintiff's factually devoid discovery responses."' (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1440.) Negligent Misrepresentation and Fraud by Nondisclosure Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to the Third and Fourth Causes of Action is GRANTED. Defendant contends there is no evidence that Plaintiffs were ever in the presence of, or communicated with Defendant, or that Defendant ever made a representation that was not true upon which Plaintiffs reasonably relied on. (Motion at p. 9.) In an action for negligent misrepresentation, a plaintiff is required to show the same elements as intentional fraud, i.e., "(1) false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure; (2) knowledge of falsity; (3) intent to defraud; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) damage," except they need not show elements two or three, i.e., "scienter or intent to defraud." (Engalla v. Permanente Medical Group, Inc. (1997) 15 Cal.4th 951, 974; Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 174.) Instead, the plaintiff is merely required to show the negligent or reckless assertion of an untrue fact. (Small, supra, 30 Cal.4th at p. 174.) (Tentative ruling continued in Part 2, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT THE GOODYEAR TIRE & RUBBER COMPANY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 6) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) Defendant also argues that Plaintiff fails to provide evidence that the alleged wrongful conduct was undertaken by an "officer, director, or managing agent." (Motion, at p.11.) Plaintiffs in opposition argue that they need not show ratification of Defendant's wrongful acts when it was Defendant's entire business enterprise which constitutes malice. (Opposition, at p. 9; C.C.P. Section 3294(b); Romo v. Ford Motor Co. (2002) 99 Cal.App.4th 1115, 1144.) The Court agrees. Plaintiffs' evidence shows that Defendant's decision to not warn or notify end users of the hazards of its products, or place asbestos related warnings or instructions on its products, were corporate decisions that are arguably attributable to its officers, directors, or managing agents. Moreover, Defendant does not dispute that its conduct occurred prior to and during the time of Decedent's exposure. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on February 11, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT THE GOODYEAR TIRE & RUBBER COMPANY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 3) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) The Court SUSTAINS Plaintiffs' Objection No. 3 to the deposition transcript of Dave Maguire (Defense Exhibit X) as inadmissible hearsay and lacking foundation. (Evidence Code Sections 1200; 1291(a), 1292(a); 401-403.) Mr. Maguire did not begin working with Defendant until 1977. As such, he lacks personal knowledge or foundation to opine to any specific facts prior to that time. The Court OVERRULES Defendant's Objections Nos. 17-36, to the cited deposition transcript portions of Joseph L. Holtshouser (Plaintiffs' Evidence D) as lacking relevance (Evid. Code, Sections 210, 350-351) and prejudicial, misleading, and likely to cause confusion. (Evid. Code, Section 352.) Mr. Holtshouser was deposed in his capacity as Goodyear's Corporate Representative, so his testimony is a party admission. The Court OVERRULES Defendant's Objection No. 37, to the cited deposition transcript portion of Ernest DeMarse (Plaintiffs' Exhibit E) as lacking relevance (Evid. Code, Sections 210, 350-351) and prejudicial, misleading, and likely to cause confusion. (Evid. Code, Section 352.) The court has not relied on the deposition testimony of Decedent or Gaviono Lloren in its analysis of Defendant's motion. "Although the clear and convincing evidentiary standard is a stringent one, it does not impose on a plaintiff the obligation to 'prove' a case for punitive damages at summary judgment or summary adjudication. Even so, where the plaintiff's ultimate burden of proof will be by clear and convincing evidence, the higher standard of proof must be taken into account in ruling on a motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication, since if a plaintiff is to prevail on a claim for punitive damages, it will be necessary that the evidence presented meet the higher evidentiary standard." (Butte Fire Cases (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1158-1159 (cleaned up).) For a corporate defendant, the oppression, fraud or malice "must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent of the corporation." (Civ. Code, Section 3294, subd. (b).) That requirement can be satisfied "'if the evidence permits a clear and convincing inference that within the corporate hierarchy authorized persons acted despicably in "willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others."' (Morgan v. J-M Manufacturing Company, Inc. (2021) 60 Cal.App.5th 1078, 1090.) A plaintiff also "can satisfy the 'managing agent' requirement through evidence showing the information in the possession of the corporation and the structure of management decision making that permits an inference that the information in fact moved upward to a point where corporate policy was formulated." (Id. at p. 1091 (cleaned up); see also Willis v. Buffalo Pumps Inc. (S.D. Cal. 2014) 34 F.Supp.3d 1117, 1134 [denying motion for summary judgment where plaintiff presented sufficient evidence that defendant's managing agents initiated and continued the sale of boilers with asbestos components and replacement parts without providing a warning of the dangers of asbestos despite knowing about those dangers, and thereby engaged in despicable conduct with a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others].) Defendant argues Plaintiffs have not produced clear and convincing evidence to show that Defendant was or should have been aware of any perceived dangerous consequences of its conduct, or that it engaged in any conduct worthy of the imposition of punitive damages. (Motion, at p. 11.) The Court disagrees. (Tentative ruling continued in Part 4, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT THE GOODYEAR TIRE & RUBBER COMPANY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 2) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) In an action for fraud by concealment or non-disclosure, a plaintiff must show the following elements: " '(1) the defendant must have concealed or suppressed a material fact, (2) the defendant must have been under a duty to disclose the fact to the plaintiff, (3) the defendant must have intentionally concealed or suppressed the fact with the intent to defraud the plaintiff, (4) the plaintiff must have been unaware of the fact and would not have acted as he did if he had known of the concealed or suppressed fact, and (5) as a result of the concealment or suppression of the fact, the plaintiff must have sustained damage." (Blickman Turkus, LP v. MF Downtown Sunnyvale, LLC (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 858, 868.) Defendant submits evidence sufficient to satisfy its burden that Plaintiffs' discovery responses are factually devoid as to evidence of negligent misrepresentation and fraud by nondisclosure. Defendant submits Plaintiffs' responses to special interrogatories, Nos 17-22 asking for "all facts" that support Plaintiffs' cause of action for negligent misrepresentation and fraud by nondisclosure. (Defense Exhibits C, E, and F.) In their response, Plaintiffs incorporate their response to the general all facts interrogatory and state that Defendant was "aware that members of the general public, and Mr. Ayers, would come into contact with their products, had no knowledge or information indicating that these products could cause injury" and "Defendant did not place any asbestos-related warnings or instructions in its asbestos-containing products." (Defense Exhibit E at 4:16-17, 21-24.) Defendant further submits deposition testimony from Decedent and Mrs. Ayers, in which Plaintiffs stipulated to not offer any testimony regarding their negligent misrepresentation and fraud causes of action. (Defense Exhibit I at 11:16-21:1; Exhibit J at 213:11-16.) Plaintiff's David and Frederick Ayers were not offered as witnesses. Moreover, Decedent's deposed coworker Gavino Lloren testified that he had no knowledge or information that Decedent ever had any communications with Defendant or supplies of products. (Defense Exhibit L at 80:17-19, 21-22; 80:23-25 and 21:2.) Here, Plaintiffs' opposition does not address these causes of action. Plaintiffs have not provided sufficient evidence to carry their burden to establish a trial issue of material fact as to these causes of action. Accordingly, the motion for summary adjudication is GRANTED as to Plaintiffs' Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation and Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Non-Disclosure. Punitive Damages Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages is DENIED. The Court OVERRULES Plaintiffs' Objections Nos. 1-2, to the cited deposition transcript portions of Ernest DeMarse (Defense Exhibit's S and W) as inadmissible hearsay. (Evidence Code Section 1292(a).) Plaintiffs object to these exhibits, arguing the transcripts are hearsay and not admissible under Berroteran v. Superior Court (2022) 12 Cal.5th 868. However, the deposition testimony of Mr. DeMarse, as Goodyear's corporate representative, comes within the exception to the hearsay rule for party admissions. Moreover, as Defendant notes, Plaintiffs provided the deposition transcripts of Mr. DeMarse in their response to production of documents. (Reply, at p. 1.) (Tentative ruling continued in Part 3, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT THE GOODYEAR TIRE & RUBBER COMPANY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 5) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) Even if Defendant had carried its initial burden, Plaintiffs have presented affirmative evidence sufficient to create a triable issue with regard to punitive damages. Plaintiffs show that Defendant had actual knowledge of the risks of mesothelioma from asbestos by 1939, and of the studies describing the risks that asbestos could cause mesothelioma. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit D at 18:24-19:5; 19:20-23; 74:24-75:17.) Plaintiffs also show that Defendant created a safety and industrial hygiene practice which were implemented by all of its subsidiaries. Defendant employed a medical director and industrial hygienist "well before 1972." (Id. at 34:17-18, 48:2-5, 49:1-8.) This is in relation to the fact that Defendant began air monitoring of asbestos in the late 60's due to OSHA's regulations regarding asbestos. (Id. at 50:3-8.) Moreover, Defendant also started air monitoring due to a concern for employees' health. (Id. at 69:9-11.) In addition, Plaintiffs show that in 1972, Defendant first informed its employees that asbestos could cause disease in humans. At this time, Defendant's medical director and industrial hygienists distributed information and trained its employees on proper procedures involving asbestos and asbestos removal. (Id. at 34:17-36:12.) However, Defendant did not warn or notify end users of the hazards of its products. (Id. at 34:17-35:23.) Lastly, Defendant did not place any asbestos related warnings or instructions on its asbestos containing products. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit E at 231:10-232:20.) Plaintiffs argue that these facts create an inference that Defendant knew about the harmful effects of asbestos. The Court agrees. Punitive damages may be available when a defendant knows the dangers of asbestos, took action to protect its own employees, knew that its products were likely to pose a danger to users, and did not warn them. (Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.Ap.4th 1270, 1300 [discussing evidence that in compliance with OSHA regulations, defendant monitored the air in its factories for asbestos particles and used engineering controls to suppress dust levels; created a safety data sheet warning its employees that exposure to asbestos caused asbestosis and cancer; knew that its products were likely to pose a danger to users, whom it did not warn; and never tested its products to determine whether their use generated concentrations of asbestos fibers exceeding the regulatory limits]; see also Stewart v. Union Carbide Corp. (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 23, 34 [substantial evidence supported jury's award of $6 million in punitive damages where it showed that "Union Carbide did not share its knowledge of the dangers of asbestos with its customers or with individuals who would, predictably, be exposed to dust from its products, and that it instead sought to downplay the risk."].) Such evidence is "sufficient to show malice, that is, despicable conduct coupled with conscious disregard for the safety of others." (Id. at pp. 1300-1301.) Given Defendant's knowledge of potential asbestos contamination of its products, and its decision to warn employees and not consumers as to the risk of asbestos exposure, a reasonable finder of fact could infer that Defendant consciously disregarded the risks of asbestos contamination of its products and failed to warn its customers and end users of those risks. (See Barenborg v. Sigma Alpha Epsilon Fraternity (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 70, 76 ["We view the evidence in the light most favorable to the nonmoving party and draw all reasonable inferences in its favor."].) (Tentative ruling continued in Part 6, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK J. AYERS JR ET AL VS. AMERICAN BILTRITE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT THE GOODYEAR TIRE & RUBBER COMPANY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 4) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) Defendant contends that the critical inquiry is what knowledge Defendant's corporate executives had regarding the health hazards of asbestos, as it related to their product, prior to the cessation of Defendant's manufacture of asbestos containing gasket material in 1969. (Motion, at p. 12; SSUMF Nos. 51-54.) However, the complaint in this action alleges that Decedent had contact with Defendant's asbestos-containing replacement gaskets, Cranite and Goodyearite, for years after 1969 (between 1961-1976). By failing to negate the possibility that Decedent was exposed to Defendant's products at all relevant times, Defendant failed to satisfy its initial burden of production. (Tentative ruling continued in Part 5, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH CURRY VS. 3M COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Specially Set A Trial Date Pursuant To C.C.P. 36 (A), (D) And (E)
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 11 2025, in Department 304, Line 2. Plaintiff's Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is conditionally GRANTED under C.C.P. section 36 (a) and (d). Oppositions filed. Joinders Filed. Reply Filed. 1. The trial date is April 14, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is June 11, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2. No later than February 21, 2025: a. Plaintiff's counsel shall make Plaintiff available for deposition. In the event he is unable to do so, Plaintiff may be barred from testifying at trial. b. Plaintiff shall provide Defendants with signed verifications of all previously-served unverified discovery responses and with all outstanding employment and medical records. Any discovery responses and documents not provided prior to February 21, 2025 may be barred from admission at trial. 3. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is April 8, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 4. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 5. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 6. The fact discovery cut-off date is March 28, 2025. 7. The expert discovery cut-off date is April 11, 2025. 8. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than March 13, 2025. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. 9. No later than February 25, 2024: a. Plaintiff's counsel shall provide Defendants with the names and contact information of any additional lay witnesses not previously identified in his discovery responses with knowledge of any facts supporting Plaintiff's claims as to each Defendant. (Gold Reply Decl. 3.) b. Defendants must provide a statement stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of facts supporting Defendant's affirmative defenses. c. Any person not identified on the statements may be barred from testifying at trial or presenting a declaration on a summary judgment motion. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)
